questions.text: The property of water which helps to maintain the integrity of the lipid bilayer is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrophobic exclusion cause water molecules to be repelled by the hydrophobic tails of phospholipids, causing them to cluster around the hydrophilic heads. This exclusion creates an environment where the hydrophobic tails are shielded from water, promoting the structural integrity of the lipid bilayer by preventing water from penetrating and disrupting its structure.

This exclusion creates an environment where the hydrophobic tails are shielded from water, promoting the structural integrity of the lipid bilayer by preventing water from penetrating and disrupting its structure.

questions.text: B 1-4 Glycosidic linkage is found in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Alpha 1-2 glycosidic linkage is present in sucrose.

Lactose, a disaccharide composed of glucose and galactose, contains a beta-1,4-glycosidic linkage. In this linkage, the hydroxyl group (-OH) on carbon 1 of glucose is connected to the hydroxyl group on carbon 4 of galactose. This beta configuration means that the oxygen atom involved in the bond formation is below the plane of the ring in the glucose molecule and above the plane in the galactose molecule. Also its a bond between beta glucose and beta galactose

Also its a bond between beta glucose and beta galactose

questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: A sequence of three nucleotides called anticodon is part of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sequence of three nucleotides called an anticodon is part of a transfer RNA (tRNA) molecule. The anticodon is complementary to a specific codon on mRNA during protein synthesis and helps to ensure the correct amino acid is brought to the ribosome during translation.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Artificial active immunity is the foundation for vaccination. It involves the activation of adaptive immunity through the deliberate exposure of an individual to weakened or inactivated pathogens, or preparations consisting of key pathogen antigens.

questions.subject: Biology

questions.text: Presence of fats in the intestine causes the release of bile under the influence of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gastrin is related to stomach so not correct option.
Acetylcholine has nothing to do with bile secretion. It is not correct option.

Secretin, on the other hand, primarily stimulates the secretion of bicarbonate-rich pancreatic juice from the pancreas. It is released by the duodenum in response to the acidity of chyme entering from the stomach, helping to neutralize it. While secretin does play a role in digestive processes, it is not directly involved in stimulating bile secretion.

The secretion of bile is primarily stimulated by the hormone cholecystokinin (CCK), rather than secretin. CCK is released by the duodenum in response to the presence of partially digested proteins and fats.

questions.explanation: The secretion of bile is primarily stimulated by the hormone cholecystokinin (CCK), rather than secretin. CCK is released by the duodenum in response to the presence of partially digested proteins and fats.

questions.text: Secondary xylem is produced by the activity of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Secondary xylem, also known as wood, is a tissue produced by the vascular cambium during secondary growth. It is made up of tracheids, vessel elements, fibers, and parenchyma cells. These cells provide mechanical support to the plant and conduct water and minerals from the roots to the leaves.

Secondary xylem, also known as wood, is a tissue produced by the vascular cambium during secondary growth. It is made up of tracheids, vessel elements, fibers, and parenchyma cells. These cells provide mechanical support to the plant and conduct water and minerals from the roots to the leaves.

questions.text: All of the following are characteristics of Chondrichthyes EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not correct because Chondrichthyes have heterocercal tail.
This is not correct because Chondrichthyes have 5 to 7 pairs of gills.
This is not correct because Chondrichthyes have cartilagenous endoskleton.

Chondrichthyes do not possess an operculum over their gills like bony fish (Osteichthyes) do. Instead, their gills are exposed directly to the water, often through multiple slits on the sides of their bodies. These gill slits are supported by cartilaginous structures rather than bony opercula.

questions.explanation: Chondrichthyes do not possess an operculum over their gills like bony fish (Osteichthyes) do. Instead, their gills are exposed directly to the water, often through multiple slits on the sides of their bodies. These gill slits are supported by cartilaginous structures rather than bony opercula.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Only one flagella arise from one side of bacteria. So it is not correct option.
The lophotrichous bacteria have several flagella located at the same place on their surface. When a tuft of flagella arises from one side of bacteria it is lophotrichous arrangement.
Flagella arise from all surface randomly , so it is not the correct option.
Flagella arise from both sides , so it is not the correct option.

questions.text: Cyclosis, the movement of cytoplasm is controlled by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is not correct option. Because they are involved in formation of centrioles.
It is not correct because intermediate filaments have other roles to do.

Cyclosis, or cytoplasmic streaming, is driven by the movement of microfilaments composed of actin and myosin proteins within the cell. These microfilaments generate force, causing the cytoplasm to flow in a circular or spiral pattern.
It is not the correct option because along with myosin action also plays its role in cyclosis.

questions.explanation: Cyclosis, or cytoplasmic streaming, is driven by the movement of microfilaments composed of actin and myosin proteins within the cell. These microfilaments generate force, causing the cytoplasm to flow in a circular or spiral pattern.

questions.subject: Biology

This is not correct because this transfer involves a third party.
It is not correct because this transfer involves conjugation bridge.

questions.text: Compare Autophagy and Autolysis.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The process by which unwanted structures within the cell are engulfed and digested within the lysosomes is called autophagy. This is self-eating process of a cell in which a lysosome begins to digest cell's own organelles. Such lysosomes are also called autophagosomes. This process either takes place in starvation period in order to obtain energy or it occurs in routine in order to control number of specific organelleWhereas, Sometimes, especially during developmental phase, when a particular cell is required to be disintegrated, a type of cell death is committed, called autolysis. This is a programmed cell death in which lysosomes burst and their enzyme contents are quickly dispersed throughout the cytoplasm. In this way the cell is disintegrated into fragments which are phagocytosed by other cells. Due to this function lysosomes are also called suicidal bags.

questions.text: Elaborate the structure of Cilia and Flagella. How does Cilia help in movement?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Cilia and flagella consists of a longitudinal axoneme. The axoneme is enclosed in a spiral sheath of cytoplasm and a plasma membrane. Axoneme is made up of a bundle of eleven longitudinal microtubules. Nine peripheral doublets are arranged in a ring. In the centre of the ring are two single microtubules. This arrangement is called "9 + 2" pattern. Cilia and flagella originate from their basal bodies embedded in the cytoplasm. Basal bodies have the same circular arrangement of microtubule triplets as centrioles. The movement of cilia is due to sliding of double fibrils in two groups one after the other. Five out of nine double fibrils contract simultaneously. As a result cilium bends or shortens. It is called effective stroke. Four out of nine double fibrils contract and cilium becomes straight. It is called recovery stroke.

questions.text: What are Stereoisomers? Why are laboratory manufactured sweeteners not metabolized in the body?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Stereoisomers are molecules that have the same molecular formula and differ only in how atoms are arranged in 3D space. Laboratory manufactured sugars are L sugars. On the other hand the naturally occurring sugars in bodies are D sugars. Proteins and cell receptors are designed to react only with D sugars. For example the enzymes in your stomach can digest only right-handed sugars. Likewise left-handed sugars cannot be metabolized by right-handed enzymes. A righthanded enzyme cannot fit on or react with left handed substrate. The substrate must fit on the proper active site of the enzyme. So for the left handed substrate (artificial sweetener) the enzyme must be left-handed.

questions.text: Justify the significance of the sequence of amino acids in Normal and Sickle Cell Haemoglobin.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Sequence of amino acid in a polypeptide is a feature of primary structure of protein which is responsible for proper functioning of protein. Even due to point mutation (change of single or few nucleotides in DNA) the sequence of amino acid in a particular protein may be disturbed which causes severe defects in the body as it happens in Sickle cell anemia. Normal haemoglobin contains four polypeptides i.e. two a-chains which consist of 141 amino acids each and two beta-chains which consist of 146 amino acids each. Sickle haemoglobin causes the cells to develop a sickle, or crescent, shape and are stiff and sticky. They tend to block blood flow in the blood vessels of the limbs and organs. Blocked blood flow can cause pain and organ damage. Sickle cell anemia is caused by a point mutation in beta-globin gene in which only one nucleotide is replaced by another which causes a change in amino acid sequence of beta-chain of haemoglobin. Sickle cell haemoglobin (HbS) shows only one difference from( HbA )i.e., glutamic acid is replaced by valine at position number six in beta chain.

questions.text: What are Prostaglandins? Mention their role in living organisms.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Prostaglandins exist in every mammalian tissue, acting as local hormones. Prostaglandins are derived from arachidonic acid. Their functions vary depending on the tissue. Some reduce blood pressure, whereas others raise it. In the immune system, various prostaglandins help to induce fever and inflammation and also intensify the sensation of pain. They also help to regulate the aggregation of platelets an early step in the formation of blood clots. In fact, the ability of aspirin to reduce fever and decrease pain depends on the inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis.

questions.text: Narrate the mechanism of Induced Fit Model of enzyme working.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: According to Induced fit model, the active site is flexible; it is modified as the substrate interacts with enzyme. The amino acids, which makeup the active site are molded into a precise shape which enables the enzyme to perform its catalytic function more effectively. The change which is induced in the shape of active site is responsible for the conversion of substrate into product. As the reaction is completed the active site regains its original shape. This is the flexibility of active site which allows more than one type of related substrates to be attached on active site and therefore, an enzyme can carry out more than one type of related reactions. The example is carbonic anhydrase which can add O2 to haemoglobin as well as can control the formation of carbonic acid and bicarbonates in blood.

questions.text: Outline the steps of Kreb's cycle with labelling.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The basic steps of Krebs cycle are as follows,Synthesis, dehydration, hydration, oxidative decarboxylation, oxidative decarboxylation and addition of CoA, formation of ATP, oxidation, hydration, oxidation.Labelled diagram is as follows.

questions.text: List the events of Photorespiration.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: When the CO2 levels inside the leaf drop to around 50 ppm (part per million), ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase (RuBisCO) starts to combine O2 with RuBP instead of CO2. The net result of this is that instead of producing two 3C molecules of phosphoglycerate (PGA), only one molecule of PGA and a toxic 2C molecule called phosphoglycolate are produced. Phosphoglycolate is converted into molecule to glycolate after removal of phosphate group. The glycolate is then transported to the peroxisome and there converted to glycine. The glycine is then transported into the mitochondria where it is converted into serine. The serine is then used to make other organic molecules

questions.text: How is Photorespiration disadvantageous?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The disadvantages of photorespiration are as follows, Reduced photosynthetic efficiency: Instead of fixing carbon dioxide, photorespiration consumes energy and releases carbon dioxide, reducing the overall efficiency of photosynthesi.Wasteful energy expenditure: Photorespiration consumes ATP without producing any useful organic molecules, leading to a net loss of energy for the plant.3. Reduction in crop yield: High temperatures and high oxygen levels make photorespiration more worse, which can significantly reduce crop yields, particularly in plants like C3 species that are more susceptible to this process.4. Photorespiration reduces the amount of carbon fixed into carbohydrate during photosynthesis by 25 percent.

questions.text: Compare characteristics of virus as non-living and living organisms.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Living Characteristics of Viruses:Reproduction: Viruses can replicate and produce multiple copies of themselves using host cell machinery.Varieties: Viruses occur in different strains and varieties.Genetic material: Viruses contain genetic material (either DNA or RNA) that carries instructions for their replication.Non-living Characteristics of Viruses:Lack of cellular structure: Viruses lack cellular structures found in living organisms, such as organelles and cytoplasm.Inability to metabolize: Viruses cannot perform metabolic processes independently and rely on host cells for energy and biosynthesis.Inert outside host cells: Viruses exist in a dormant state outside host cell.

questions.text: The diagram shows HIV.a) Name the parts labelled as A, and Bb) Which cells are affected by HIV?c) List Opportunistic diseases caused by HIV infection
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: a) Part A is Reverse Transcriptase enzyme. Part B is glycoprotein.b) HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) primarily targets cells of the immune system, specifically T Lymphocytes(CD4) cells, which are a type of white blood cells. HIV enters these cells and replicates within them, leading to their destruction.c) Opportunistic diseases caused by HIV include Kaposi's sarcoma( cancer or lesion on skin), pneumonia, eye infections, fungal infections and nervous system disorders.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Gram positive bacteriaThickness : 20 to 80nmPeptidoglycan: more peptidoglycanPeriplasmic space: present in someGram negative bacteriaThickness: 8 to 11 nmPeptidoglycan: less peptidoglycanPeriplasmic space: present in all

questions.text: Differentiate photosynthesis in Cyanobacteria and Bacteria.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis in bacteria typically uses bacteriochlorophylls and does not produce oxygen.Bacteria do not use water as reducing agent.Some bacteria do not use light energy instead they use energy from oxidation of inorganic substances. Cyanobacteria perform photosynthesis with chlorophyll a, producing oxygen as a byproduct .Cyanobacteria use water as reducing agent.Cyanobacteria use light energy and carbon dioxide.

questions.text: List and elaborate any six salient features of Protozoa.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Protozoa are single-celled eukaryotic organisms that exhibit various characteristics. Here are six main features of protozoa: 1.Unicellular: Protozoa are composed of a single cell, which carries out all necessary functions for life. 2. Motility: Many protozoa are capable of movement using specialized structures such as cilia, flagella, or pseudopodia.3. Cyst formation: Protozoa form resistant cyst to overcome unfavorable conditions. 4. Reproduction: Protozoa reproduce both sexually and asexually. 5. Habitat: Protozoa are found in diverse habitats, including freshwater, marine environments, soil, and the bodies of other organisms.6.Regeneration :Protozoa have the ability to regenerate.

questions.text: Write about cause, prevention and treatment of Dyspepsia.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Incomplete or imperfect digestion is called dyspepsia.Cause: It may occur due to acidity in stomach or faulty function of stomach and intestine or insufficient secretion of bile.Prevention: Avoid food that worsens symptoms. Stop smoking., weight reduction , small meals, avoid alcohol, fatty food, heavy lifting, late night meals to reduce reflex during sleep.Treatment Antibiotics should be taken. Drugs which decrease HCL production such as Cimetidine; stop NSAID (Non-Steroidal Anti Inflammatory Drugs),e.g. Aspirin.

questions.text: Differentiate between Protostomes and Deuterostomes.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Protostomes have spiral and determinate cleavage.In protostomes, blastopore develops into mouth .Coelom is schizocoelom which is formed by splitting of mesoderm.Deuterostomes have radial and indeterminate cleavage.In deuterostomes, blastopore develops into anus.Coelom is enterocoelom which is formed by out pocketing of primitive gut.

questions.text: Write about structure of a typical antibody.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are Y-shaped proteins composed of four polypeptide chains—two heavy chains and two light chains—linked by disulfide bonds. Each chain has variable (V) and constant (C) regions. The two arms of the Y-shaped structure contain antigen-binding sites, which recognize and bind specific antigens. Antibodies are transported through blood and lymph to pathogen invasion site.

questions.text: List the adaptations in Xerophytes which enable them to live in that specific environment.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Xerophytes are plants adapted to live in dry or arid environments. Their adaptations allow them to thrive despite limited water availability. Some adaptations of xerophytes include:Reduced leaf surface: Xerophytes often have reduced leaf surface area to minimize water loss through transpiration.Thick cuticle: They have a thick waxy cuticle covering the leaf surface, reducing water loss through evaporation.Sunken stomata: Stomata, the tiny pores on leaves, are often sunken into pits or grooves to reduce water loss by creating a more humid microenvironment around the stomatal openings.Hairy or waxy leaf surfaces: Some xerophytes have hairy or waxy leaf surfaces, which can trap moisture and reduce transpiration.

questions.text: Inflammatory response is a type of Non-Specific Defence. State its events.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Inflammatory response, a nonspecific defense mechanism, involves events such as vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, leukocyte migration, phagocytosis, release of inflammatory mediators, and tissue repair. Migration of leukocytes: White blood cells, particularly neutrophils and macrophages, migrate to the site of injury.Phagocytosis: Phagocytes engulf and destroy pathogens, cellular debris, and foreign substances.Release of inflammatory mediators: Macrophages, and other cells release inflammatory mediators such as histamine, prostaglandins, and cytokines attract more immune cells to the site of infection.Tissue repair: Inflammatory cytokines stimulate tissue repair mechanisms.

questions.text: Write down the principle of Angioplasty.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The principle of angioplasty involves using a balloon catheter to widen narrowed or obstructed blood vessels, typically arteries. During the procedure, the balloon is inserted into the affected artery and inflated to compress plaque buildup, restoring blood flow. This technique helps alleviate symptoms and reduce the risk of complications associated with arterial blockages. Catheter is threaded into arm or leg and then guided through blood vessel toward heart.

questions.text: What are Irreversible Non-Competitive Inhibitors? Describe with examples.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Irreversible noncompetitive inhibitors are molecules that permanently bind to an enzyme. Irreversible non-competitive enzyme inhibitor destroys enzyme by altering its shape so that the substrate cannot bind to the active site. The examples of irreversible non-competitive inhibitors include cyanides and salts of heavy metals. Cyanides are potent poisons of living organism because they can kill an organism by inhibiting cytochrome oxidase essential for cellular respiration. They block the action of these enzymes by combining with iron which may be present in the prosthetic group. Ions of heavy metals such as mercury, silver and copper (Hg++ , Ag+ , and Cu++) combine with thiol (-SH) groups in the enzyme breaking the disulphide bridges. These bridges are important in maintaining tertiary structure. When these bridges are broken, the enzyme becomes denatured.

questions.text: Describe Life Cycle of a Fern. Also Sketch and label the life cycle.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Life cycle of fern is as follows,Sporangia on sporophyte produce haploid spores through meiosis.Spores are dispersed and germinate into haploid gametophytes.Gametophytes produce male (antheridia) and female (archegonia) gametangia.Sperm produced by antheridia fertilize eggs in archegonia.Fertilization results in the formation of a diploid zygote.Zygote develops into a new sporophyte, completing the cycle.

questions.text: Describe Mutualism established in Mycorrhizae and Lichen Association.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Mutualism in lichens and mycorrhizae refers to symbiotic relationships where both organisms benefit.In lichens, a mutualistic association between a fungus (usually an ascomycete) and an algae or cyanobacteria exists. The fungus provides a protective structure and absorbs water and nutrients, while the photosynthetic partner produces sugars through photosynthesis, benefiting both organisms.In mycorrhizae, mutualism occurs between fungi and the roots of plants. The fungus extends the root system's reach, enhancing nutrient uptake, particularly phosphorus and nitrogen, while the plant provides the fungus with carbohydrates produced through photosynthesis. This symbiotic relationship is essential for many plants to thrive in various ecosystems.

questions.text: Write General Characteristics of Phylum Arthropoda.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Arthropods have following characteristics.They have exoskeleton made of chitin.They have segmented body with distinct regions: head, thorax, abdomen.They have jointed appendages for movement and specialized functions.They have bilateral symmetry.They have open circulatory system.They show metamorphosis in different species.

questions.text: How blood flow through Heart is regulated by Valves?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Valves regulate blood flow in heart in following ways,Atrioventricular valves (tricuspid and mitral) prevent backflow from ventricles to atria during ventricular contraction.Pulmonary semilunar valves prevent backflow from pulmonary trunk to ventricle during ventricular relaxation.Aortic semilunar valves prevent backflow from aorta to ventricle during ventricular relaxation.Valves open and close in response to pressure changes in the heart chambers.Chordae tendineae and papillary muscles support valve function.

questions.text: In Systemic circulation, write about circulation of blood to heart, liver and kidneys.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: In systemic circulation, blood is pumped from the left ventricle of the heart through the aorta and distributed throughout the body.Heart: Oxygenated blood is pumped from the left ventricle through the aorta, supplying oxygen and nutrients to the heart muscle itself through the coronary arteries.Liver: Oxygenated blood from the hepatic artery and nutrient-rich, deoxygenated blood from the portal vein mix within the liver sinusoids.Kidneys: Oxygenated blood from the renal artery enters the kidneys, where waste products are filtered out and excess ions and water are regulated to maintain electrolytes and blood is then returned via the renal veins.

questions.text: Explain structure of a Villus. How is its structures well-suited for absorption of different digested food such as glucose, amino acids and fats?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: A villus is a small finger-like projection found in the small intestine's lining, particularly in the jejunum and ileum. It plays a crucial role in the absorption of nutrients during digestion. The total area of absorption becomes incredibly large due to the enfolding, villi and microvilli. Simple Sugars and amino acids are absorbed by diffusion or active transport into the blood capillaries through the microvilli. Some of the fatty acids and glycerol are also absorbed into blood stream. Epithelial cells , lacteal and capillaries together play role to make villi effective for absorption.

questions.text: How does K+ ions influx and efflux control opening and closing of stomata?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Stomata open due to active transport of potassium ions (K+ ) into the guard cells from the surrounding epidermis. The accumulation of K+ decreases the osmotic potential of guard cells. Water enters the guard cells by osmosis, which become more turgid and stretched and stomata are opened. The stoma closes by reverse process; involving passive diffusion of K+ from guard cells followed by water moving out by osmosis. Level of carbon dioxide in the spaces inside the leaf and light, control this movement. A low level of carbon dioxide favors opening of the stomata, thus allowing an increased carbon dioxide level and increased rate of photosynthesis.

questions.text: The organelle which will be more active in liver cells after alcohol intake is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No it is not correct option because lysosomes are involved in autophagy and autolysis.

The peroxisome is more active in liver cells after alcohol intake due to its role in alcohol metabolism. Peroxisomes contain enzymes like catalase and alcohol oxidase that are involved in the breakdown of ethanol (alcohol) into acetaldehyde and then into acetate, which can be further metabolized by other cellular processes.
No it is not correct because glyoxisomes is present in germinating seeds.
No it is not correct because polysome is related to ribosomes that is related to protein synthesis.

Peroxisomes contain enzymes like catalase and alcohol oxidase that are involved in the breakdown of ethanol (alcohol) into acetaldehyde and then into acetate, which can be further metabolized by other cellular processes.
